speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three in the interview with Maria Glickman. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after they killed the wife and husband, we were already to the right side. My girlfriend came out with her daughter, three and 1/2 years old. So three-- two Ukrainian SS-- because our ghetto [? closed ?] the Ukrainian SS, not the German-- took the child for both legs, in half, and up the wall. She was three and 1/2 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What became of the mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother stayed alive. And we were in [? chimney ?] together. We will get free together. She just passed away this year. And I didn't say about my father. First of all, my father has written 500 [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a Torah. It means not the Torah that we have now, but in the same, in the same style in Hebrew, about what's going on from the beginning up to the end before we left the ghetto, what's going on in the ghetto, what the Germans did, what the Jewish people did not [? right, ?] who got killed, why they got killed, everything. And they close it up. And they took it to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in the, in the cellar. When I was in '56, the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was there. And I came in '93, no more [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a new house. And I don't know. I think this must be under the ground. Doesn't matter. I hope somebody will find, because there is written the date and his name and his family and the date when he finished. So when he finished it, two or three days later, I took them to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], to go out from the ghetto. They wait for-- not for him, for many people-- to take him to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he picked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But he was Jewish, not another thing. And he was 40-- when they took them, he was 42 and 1/2 years old. When they killed him, he was almost 44. They took them. And he got typhus. This I know from [? Advocat ?] Muszkat, the, the teacher. He was with him together. And he tell me this. Otherwise, I wouldn't. He came to our concentration camp. And he said your father had typhus. He's very weak. I hope everything will go all right, but we-- you never know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you said to your father when he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember. I said that I think that this is the last time I see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. He just did say nothing. But I saw his eyes were full. He want to cry, but he hold himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have any other jobs or responsibilities in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had the job to paint for the ghetto. He got food for it, a quarter of a pound food a week, like butter. Otherwise, you hadn't seen it-- like six eggs, bread, warm bread, some stuff. So we ate it together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he do anything for the rabbis? Did he have anything to do with--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. This he did for main rabbi from ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, the book that he wrote?

speaker: interviewee
text: The book, yes. That wasn't a book. It did go like this, around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a Torah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like a Torah. And the rabbi called it Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He called it a Torah? Were there-- was there any religious activity in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had a yeshiva. We had synagogue. People were-- most of the people were kosher. We didn't get kosher food. But they hold it kosher. Kosher They took not the kosher and make it kosher, because we didn't have other one. We didn't get meat. We get meat the last year-- horse meat. We did get margarine, frozen potatoes. Bread was like a stone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any holiday celebrations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every holiday, they came to kill people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is-- the yellow, this is from a song against the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when you wear it. And the end in the song is I help that I will live free. Because not me are wearing it, you will wear it later. And then it was five Germans in the theater when this was sung.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say a word. We were scared. But they didn't say a word, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the song was sang, even though there were Germans there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They ca-- they had to come. Always when it was a concert, they came to see what's going on. They are themselves were not nice people. So how can they believe people who they too thinked we are not nice. We are terrible people. Nobody did nothing. And then I forgot to tell you, I worked before they took me to the concentration camp. I think I telled you. I worked for [INAUDIBLE] We maked the trains to go from Vilno to Berlin. When I came out from jail,. A couple of-- two months later, a month [INAUDIBLE], they took me there. And I worked up to the end of September. I worked in three cities-- [INAUDIBLE] When you finished in one place, they took [? like to the work. ?] It was terrible. We sleeped where the horses sleep. The food, all over the same. If we stood and we worked and it was raining, we got to sleep in the wet stuff. If we stand up, we would have to go again with the wet stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only thing that I had better than oth-- some other people is because I sp-- I was the only one in this working place who speak German. So the-- how was his name? I don't remember, but the main person from the place was German. And he couldn't speak another language. So you need me. So you didn't beat me up. And this was the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What does [INAUDIBLE] mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Oh, god. This is a organization for an older people who can't go to the army and help-- have to help out the German army. So they worked with people who, who are not worth nothing. And where they send them, they have to work. This was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the third round up came, the third [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: The third [INAUDIBLE]-- I didn't go to work two weeks after-- I was in a, in a cellar, I told you, in a cellar. There was a store. And, and they had a bookstore. And there wasn't books. But the cellar had some old books. And we were there-- two woman with a child, and another woman with  a man, and me-- six people, six week. Yeah. We were scared to go out. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before the third [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the time from third [INAUDIBLE]. And we didn't walk out, because we were scared. But one night I said, I have to go to see my father. And I walked out. And it was very quiet. And I got to know that the [INAUDIBLE] had already finished long ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came home. We did go to our home. And the lady-- two ladies with the child did go to their home. And then it was the [INAUDIBLE] that they took us to the concentration camp-- the, the 24,000 people and 400 children, 22,000 people to Majdanek, and 2,000 people to Kaiserwald, Riga.

speaker: interviewee
text: Between the people was 400 children. They tooked out the children from the mothers' hand. They put them in one place. And everybody had to walk through the children alive. Because you couldn't go back. So you go near to the [INAUDIBLE], you know, to a route. And, and the children were dumped. It-- this was terrible, to walk over children alive. And in that moment, I heard somebody called my name and said, goodbye. I saw my girlfriend hanging. They-- she want to run away. And they catched her and three, three men. They catched them all. And they hanged them. And she could say goodbye.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was all as the people were exiting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wa-- walking to the exit out. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children? People just trampled over them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They trampled. The children were dead. Not one child was alive. How do I know? Because when, when I was in '46 in Vilno, I did go to the place. And there was a lady who was in the time that they tooked us. And she telled me that not one child was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were part of the 2,000 [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the 2,000. To the concentration camp, we came just 1,700. 300 run through the windows. The windows were closed where they took away all the, the, the wood and everything. And they threw themselves out to the running train. And 300 didn't go on with us. We were just 1,700 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took you by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, but they took us so that it took them three and 1/2 days and three nights. They didn't want people to see, so-- because one thing, you have to go out. You are alive. You must go. So where they-- early, early in the morning when everybody was asleep, they would take us out and put us back. But what I have seen is in one train where I was, there was a mother with a daughter. And Ukrainian SS came in. I didn't tell you how they cau-- how terrible they close the ghetto. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can. Should I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came. They closed it in two days. They came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like-- what can I tell you-- Back to back, two soldiers. And then one here and one the other. And from all four places, they shot where it did go. Doesn't matter where. They walked in shooting. So people run. Some people got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they, they didn't do like the German SS, slowly, you know? They catched you by your hair. They give you with the feet here or somewhere else. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The new SS. This was Ukrainian soldiers who run away from Ukraine, got to the Germans. They were two years closed up like us. And then they took them out. And they send them to the SS. And they gave them the, the, the SS [INAUDIBLE]. And they were SS. But they were, in their behavior, terrible, much, much worser than, than the SS, the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they were Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: They spoke Ukraine. They couldn't speak German. Could be some could, but not all of them. So they-- so they closed the ghetto like this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after three days and, and-- three and 1/2 days and three nights, in September 27, we came near middle of the night to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43-- September '43, 27.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the conditions on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ter-- yes. I was-- everybody was in the train, where it was 80 people. It was terrible. You couldn't sit. You couldn't stand. It, it-- so when they're stopped to take out people to go out came two Ukrainian with lamps. And they took the mother and the daughter. They didn't take them out-- took them in aside. And they did what they did. And I saw it. And my girlfriend, who lives in Sweden said to me, Maria, we have to run. I says, yes. So I walked out. And I looked for another train. And I find a train. They called them [INAUDIBLE] train. We closed 12 girls inside. And nobody could open it. And thank to this, I think we came to Kaiserwald. Otherwise, they would to us what they did to other ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: What became of the women on that other train, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came to Kaiserwald. And she was very, very scared. She asked a German-- I was standing there. And she said, please, sir-- In Jewish, she couldn't speaking the German. Please, would you please kill me? I can't stand.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what are you talking about? We don't kill people here. She said, but you have a gun. You can kill me. He said, no. You go in your barrack. Barrack, that mean the place where we sit. I don't kill people [? here with this. ?] And why is it smelling? So he said we make the disinfection so nobody will get sick. It's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then it was the [INAUDIBLE]. They-- I don't know why, but they think that everybody who comes must have lice. Nobody did. But they tooked 40, 40 people in one time. I was from the 41st people. And I was [INAUDIBLE] It means they took away my lice. They took away my, my, my dress, my everything. And they gave me to, to wear a sweater with long sleeves-- here, everything open, a short skirt. And I had my own, my own shoes. And I had 16 year not cut my hair, just the ends. And the commandant, the, the-- he was, he was sitting in, in concentration camp, too. His name was Rosenmeyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came. And he said, where are you? Are you from the crazy house? I say, why are you saying it? He says, you look like one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I got this stuff from you people. He said, they not mine people. I'm a [INAUDIBLE] like you. I-- that means I sit in the concentration camp like you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, well, we think you are a little bit better. You're not Jewish. So he said to me, come. I will give you what to wear. And he-- which one is your package? I said, I have two.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, take one. So I tooked one. And I got dressed. I changed [INAUDIBLE], because I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to put your own clothes on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And then they send us to another place in concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had you arrived to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Kaiserwald. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Riga, but it wasn't in the city. It was outside the city. But the whole place is called Kaiserwald. Later, I lived there, 12 1/2 years. The, the whole city-- the whole place was called Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was an enclosed camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cl-- the camp was separate-- but in the beginning from the, from the place. Then later, there was just villas-- no houses, no, no, no little house-- villas, beautiful villas.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they took you into a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us in, everybody, to the camp. And we had to work. And I remember the first time they send me to work to SS [INAUDIBLE] [? Rutenburg. ?] This is a, a, a hospital for the SS called [? Rutenburg. ?] And they telled us to sing [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Jewish blood is running from the knife, they will win the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to sing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we must do. And there, I worked all over. First, I tooked out nails from all the wood. Then we opened all the houses. Then we take the wood in another place. Then they send me to wash toilets, to, to, to wash steps, everything. And then the, the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- that means the chef. He was a officer-- came and asked who speak German. So we were two girls. One is in Israel. I said, I speak German.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, would you want to work with the sick people? I said, may, may I say yes or no? He said, you can saw what you want. I understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said yes, I would want. At least I will be warm. So I worked there. And the soldiers brought me eight sandwiches a day. But I didn't eat myself. We eat, eight people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sick soldier. They were-- who came to, to get healed. They were shot, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were German soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: German soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food-- eight sand-- eight people, eight sandwiches a day. And I gave it to eight people, because my girlfriend had a son. He was 14 years old. She was my girlfriend in, in that time, because she was much older than I. And I gave for him. He lives in Los Angeles. He's a engineer, very nice man. This was the first place where I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had-- the food was ample at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yes. Then they sent us to [INAUDIBLE]. This is the first place for the, for the German-- how do you say flyers? The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pilots.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pilots, the officers-- And we worked there. First, I-- we cleaned the potatoes, the bad in one side, the good one in other side. Then they sent me in a villa to the colonel to clean the villa-- to wash the windows, wash the toilets, clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a Latvian lady. She stole food from him. And when he, he saw that there is not enough food, he asked her. She said, that's not me. That's the two Jewish girl. So [INAUDIBLE]. So he asked me to come to his room. And he said, how about food? I say, what about food? We get once a day bread and soup. Would you know who stole the bread and the cake what I get from Germany? I said no. I really didn't know. So he gave me his key. He said, when you hungry, you go and you take food. He was an old man. He would play on a [? flight. ?] You know, a [? flight? ?] He gave me food. And the young lady out, because he understood that she did it. Do you recall his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I I remember he was a, a, a colonel. He was a very intelligent man. He was playing music by notes. That means he was something in his head. But I can't remember his name. I see him. He had big glasses on. But his name, I don't remember. And then they send me back to work again in [INAUDIBLE]. What they said that we do-- we were not the bosses. We did everything that men supposed to do. Oh, they send us to bring in blocks for houses. And it was January or February. It was very cold. And we stood on the ocean into one block. And I froze my both feet here. Because I didn't have what to wear. It was cold. There, I worked. Then they send me to another place called [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE], that means the main boss of the place. He was aristocrat, really. He didn't see us. Me and my girlfriend from Sweden, he send us to cut trees. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. [LAUGHS] We didn't-- first, of all, we were scared. It was snow up to here. And to cut trees, this is for men work.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she said, [INAUDIBLE], you know what? Don't talk about it. We will try. And we tried.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a man came. He said when the-- you look how the tree is going, to the right or to the left. Otherwise, you get killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, this [INAUDIBLE] came. And he said, this is not work for girls. This will do the men. And you come inside and work-- clean the offices. Work where you need to be working, not there. So this was a very nice time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I worked in another place called [INAUDIBLE]. There were [INAUDIBLE]. They weren't SS, just people who brought us there were the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a Latvian man called Pukitis. And he want to do to me. And I-- in the beginning, I think he's, he's making a joke. And then he said, lie down. Otherwise, I will shot you. And in that moment came Pukitis. And he said, what are you doing? He said, why not? He said, you German. You can't touch a Jewish girl. It's not clean. And he make to me like this. He means so he will not touch me. So he didn't. And Pukitis was an SS officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He was a private man who had this place. And they took it from him, but he was head boss. This was Pukitis. His name, I remember. Some names, you can't always remember names.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you at Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kaiserwald, I was from Sept-- '43, September 27, in the middle of the night to August the third. They send me to a small concentration camp called Strassenhoff. In Lithuanian, it was [LITHUANIAN SPEECH]. It's easier to say it's Strassenhoff. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Riga-- not in the city-- away from the city. There I was-- from there, I got free. We got free October 13, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your experience there in Strassenhoff.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- when I came, it was terrible. Because they tooked me-- I didn't want to go, but nobody-- what, what could I say? I did want to go together with my girlfriend, but she worked. So I had to go alone. And I cried, of course, because I didn't know what was waiting for us there. And when I came, the wait nothing. 50 girls from there did go back. From the 50, three stayed in Kaiserwald. And me and two more sent back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Strassenhoff?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Strassenhoff, to clean up the concentration camp before they leave the place. Because the Russian were already under the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to break and go to the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.
